rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MANUEL CRUZ MARTIR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To First Amended Petition Of Administrator Manuel Martir, Jr. To Determine Ownership Of Estate Property And For Order Authorizing And Directing The Transfer Of Estate Property To Claimant Manuel Martir, Jr., Individually
rulings.Rulings: The demurrer to the First Amended Petition is sustained without leave to amend. A long line of cases beginning with Norton v. Bassett (1908) 154 Cal. 411, 415-16) and continuing through Martin v. Kehl (1983) 145 Cal.App. 3d 228, 240 establishes that a voluntary resulting trust becomes an involuntary constructive trust by operation of law upon the voluntary trustee's death. These cases hold that an involuntary constructive trust, created by operation of law following the death of a voluntary trustee, is inherently adverse to the beneficiary's interests and automatically begins the statutory limitations period running at the voluntary trustee's death without the requirement of the constructive trustee's repudiation of the trust. Estate of Yool (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 867 is not to the contrary. The issue on appeal in Yool was whether the one-year statute of limitations under CCP section 366.2 applied to the petitioner's constructive trust claim. Yool's analysis turned on whether a personal claim existed against the decedent voluntary trustee as of the time of death. Such a claim could have been triggered if the decedent had repudiated his trust obligations. Had such a claim existed, there would have been a pre-death personal claim against decedent that, in turn, could have triggered the section 366.2 limitation period. "[A]t the time of Yool's death [petitioner] had suffered no harm." Id. At 877. Yool's discussion of repudiation is pre-death, emphasizing that the issue of who owned the property "only arose after Yool's death." Id. Therefore, since there was no pre-death claim arising from the pre-death resulting trust, section 366.2 was not triggered. The court did not consider or address whether the four-year statute of limitations in section 343 applied or began to run, nor would it have had occasion to consider that issue since the claim in that case was brought within four years of the voluntary trustee's death. Because the petition in this case was brought well beyond the four-year limitations period, the petition is barred by section 343. In reaching this conclusion, the Court does not consider any other claims Petitioner may have with respect to the property or his investments in it. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, November 20, 2019.

rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR APPOINTMENT OF CONSERVATOR OF PERSON AND ESTATE
